speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, Norbert Kerenyi. You were telling me about the fascist hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's a very interesting place. The whole concept was very interesting. The reason for having this at all was that the more intelligent fascists, by the fall of 1944, had seen quite clearly that the war is over and they lost. They still though, were believing their own propaganda. And this chief of the Sixth District-- the fascist chief of the Sixth District-- really believed that when the Russian armies came under the commandership of Jewish generals, then his life would be spared. Because he saved around 500, 600 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he was a very intelligent man. He was an engineer and university-trained. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeretzyan-Ara, George. George being the first name. Yeretzyan-Ara was his family name, and he was Armenian in origin. And he decided to organize this hospital. This hospital was staffed by the best Jewish doctors in Budapest. Who was organized by one of the people who lived in that house. His name was [? Bergessi ?]. He was a psychiatrist. And he was a-- a person who had a very large practice. He was very interesting man. I would not say that he was a terribly pleasant man and one of my bad experiences are attached to him-- but I-- if we have time I may come back to this later. I'll just continue now with the hospital, which was organized and staffed by these Jewish physicians. We had approximately 25 different specialties there, and initially they were seeing patients as they came in. It was a-- a [? ambulance ?] service for those who came. And of course it was free. I managed to see one of the submissions of this Mr. Yeretzyan-Ara who offered to the Minister of Interior that he will organize a hospital like this in every district in Budapest. Because as he noted, the Jewish possessions are national treasure. And the procession of Jewish doctors is their knowledge. And this, he could put into service for the Hungarian people. But there was no time to organize all this so he just organized this one. Now of course, in addition to the doctors, there was need for a supporting staff. And this is how we got in. Now the hospital initially had a very small number of beds. Around 100 beds, or even less. But as the war continued, we developed a major role in treating the wounded. And there were many wounded people, civilian and from the military. And we were growing-- the supply came from the fascist storage headquarter. And we were reasonably supplied with food-- what they had. And of course, this mainly went to him and to the doctors, who were, of course, considered to be the most important. I was very much impressed as I had nothing, really, to eat. Nothing very substantial. There was, up until the last 10 days, there was a central kitchen, where they had some food for the members of the staff. But [INAUDIBLE] through this [? Bergessi ?] told me that he has somewhere-- about three kilometers from this place where we were in the basement of a house, two big sticks of salami. And if I bring it to him, he will give me one. So I went during the siege, and you could imagine that of course [INAUDIBLE] was not an easy thing. I got there, I found it, and I brought it back. And he told me that he changed his mind. And he doesn't give me from the salami. But instead of that, he will give me his book that he wrote, that the soul is everything, and he will autograph it for me. But I told him that he can keep his book. I am not really interested in that. Give me half. He didn't give me anything. So I immediately decided that if I ever will be in such a position again, I will tell whoever will send me-- I don't think that I will have another opportunity-- that I found nothing and keep everything to myself. But I didn't have that much brain at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is just a minor episode. The important thing is that this hospital grew into significance. And we also got stronger in this hospital. We had a very influential man, who was a commander, who somehow managed to get weapons. Members of the Hungarian army and even Germans were selling weapons. And he had some contacts who were buying. So we had a small, armed group there, which was our resistance, prepared to resist any attack on our lives. We really had no dreams of attacking the German army. We felt that the second and third Ukrainian army that was attacking the German army was more than adequate to accomplish this task.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at one point, when we were practicing in the basement, our commander came in. And he-- we were absolutely frightened. We didn't know what to do. We cannot shoot him because then we are finished. And so he discovered us. But he wasn't unfriendly. He said that he's glad that we have this-- this preparedness. And he said we should not talk to anybody about this, not even members of our own family. And [INAUDIBLE], because before the front comes over us, in his opinion, there will be a commando of fascists to kill this house. Because they have a standing order not to allow any Jew to survive if they evacuate any place. And he said that for that opportunity, if he cannot order them away, then we can defend the house. And we worked out a very good plan that he had his study where he accepted everybody who came to him. And his desk was reinforced by steel blades on the top and on the side. And he placed us in strategic position all over. He studied. The door was always left open in this. And he said that if he cannot convince them to leave, then he will light a cigarette, at which point we should start to shoot at them and defend the house. Because otherwise, everybody will get killed. And we agreed to this task. And fortunately, they really came. There were around 15 fascist soldiers, very well-equipped, who came to execute the Jews in the house. And he managed to tell them that they cannot do that. There are brothers in here-- wounded brothers-- whom they care for, and they cannot kill their own brothers. And they will get killed if we don't take care of them. And they should go to the front and fight the Russian rather than, you know, spend bullets on valueless Jews. And he convinced them. He didn't light the cigarette, so we didn't have that confrontation. But we had one. And this is the only one in which I participated a military style of confrontation. One of us was captured. Somebody must have recognized him because we had excellent papers. He was captured by the fascists and was taken to the Fifth District headquarter. And our commander felt that if he's tortured he might tell where we are and who we are. And he said that we have to break him out. And of course, there were only 40 there who was armed. And we needed some more help. We estimated that there are at least 400 fascist soldiers in the district headquarter. And he had his contacts. So by the evening, there was another 50 people from another underground group, well-armed. And another 40. So all together we were 130. And we decided that at midnight we shall attack them. And I'm very proud to have participated in the planning. Because the question was how to approach the headquarter. There were outposts where they were checking people who are moving into that area. So we agreed that we had uniforms, uniforms of the Air Defense units. And the uniforms had fascist paraphernalia, fascist armbands and fascist hat. And we surrounded the others. And they had their arms under their winter coat, who were in civilian clothes. I told them that they are Jews who we are taking to the headquarter. And everybody let us through. We had-- the uniform had a major significance to most people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived there, and there were only four guards at the door. This was an apartment house that was converted to the fascist headquarter. And our commander, who was very well-trained in commando exercises, asked for volunteers who would kill them with a knife. You know this silent kill is not discovered by [? Simpson ?]. This was a standard commando method. And he couldn't find four volunteers. And he really got angry that we are coward, yellow-bellied groups. And I had my brightest idea. I told him, why don't we shoot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, there was no reason to kill them silently. There was a constant artillery barrage. We could hardly hear each other. So four more shots. And he said that I am right, and we managed to shoot them. And we managed to cause tremendous damage in the Fifth District headquarter. We lost eight to nine people in [INAUDIBLE], but I don't how many-- their losses were over 100. And we managed to get to this boy, whom we can-- could not take out. Actually, his order was either take him out or kill him, if we cannot take him out out. If he's so seriously injured, he was dead. So they tortured him to death. And he evidently didn't say anything. And we left him. We came out. And the next day, there were still papers published. They said that Russian commandos attacked the Fifth District headquarter. But they were repelled with heavy losses. And it was a good deal of satisfaction to know that it wasn't three Russians commandos. And nobody repelled us. Fortunately, they were all sleeping or drunk. So we managed to break through them and we had about 35 hand grenades. We used most of them in throwing to their sleeping quarters. And it was a very satisfactory event. And that was the only one in which I participated. And this was not even known to members of the house-- that we went through this. Most of them did not know that we provided a final defense for them. Because we were not able to speak. And one of the reasons why I wanted to say this because I think this should be remembered. That was sometimes an opportunity to-- to fight back. And when it came, we used this opportunity.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after this, we still had at least 12, 14 days to survive. And the situation became-- as the Russians came closer and closer, more and more tight-- our supplies were difficult to get. Russian aeroplanes were attacking any vehicle that moved on the street. They reached in such a stage that they were the approximately four kilometers from the place where we were. And then they went to the supplies. They were already shooting on the street-- on that street where we had to cross. So this gave me an opportunity to-- to ask my friend with whom I went-- you know we were seeking shelter in a house and we had to cross that street, and they were shooting fairly regularly with a machine gun. And I asked him what is more polite, if I let you go ahead or I go ahead? He told me that, under the circumstances, it would be more polite if I would go ahead. So I managed to get through. I pulled a tricycle through, which was hit several times. But the tricycle didn't mind. And then he also got through. So we managed to-- to get back to the supply. But these were the last 10 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And was another episode, which was rather hair-raising-- a friend of mine with whom we went for a bakery shop was recognized by a fascist. And they told him that, I recognize you, you are a Jew and I'll arrest you. So he said, you just think so. And in the next moment, he shot him. And immediately called the fascists-- they were all over-- that he is a Jew, I recognized him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to make a sign, put it on him, that this is the fate of Jews who try to hide. And we decided never to go into that area again. During this time, you were with false papers, am I right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I was with my own paper. I had false papers and I used them before. But there-- you know, one of the most embarrassing situations was that this chief, who had the power to provide me-- me a certificate of the Hungarian Fascist Party, he produced a certificate that, the presenter of the certificate Comrade Norbert Kohn. He's serving the Sixth District Fascist Party. And as such, he's very important for the war effort and you should support him, wherever he goes. And then he closed it with a stamp-- that fascist greeting. And I was a little bit concerned that Comrade, or Brother Norbert Kohn may not sit very well other fascist units. So I asked him to make it on another name. And he asked me what name do you want. So I told him what name I want and I got a paper with that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kardush. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my uncle, who was a hero of the First World War, elected to have that name. That was quite voluntary. He was a Hungarian patriot. And his name was [? Kardush ?]. And I thought that it's already occurring in the family. And that was the first name that came to my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: At another time you told me you took-- a friend arranged false papers for you. Michael--

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael [? Bolob ?]. Actually I used his papers to move around. Indeed, it was very important-- very [? helpful ?] when I went into this fascist hospital, when I was-- when it was established. I couldn't move on the street as a Jew. I had to move with false papers. And his false papers gave me security. And I was particularly lucky to have false papers that were real, because he gave me his own papers. And as I told you when we talked in our primary discussion, at one point I managed to convince him to join the Hungarian Fascist Party with my photograph. And I had a authenticated fascist certificate that I'm a member of the Hungarian Fascist Party. And this helped me out of many difficult situations.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, were you afraid that you might not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. No, I-- Of course I was afraid because when you-- if you ever have the immediate danger that you are losing your life, you are always afraid. It's a very traumatizing experience. But I always thought that somehow I will manage. And even with totally impersonal things, such as air raids or artillery barrages, or shooting, I always thought that somehow I will manage. But of course, I was afraid. I was very much afraid before we attacked this house a whole afternoon while I was waiting to have direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that liberation was approaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard it. You know, this-- the Russian artillery was very loud. And it was coming closer and closer. So I knew that it's coming. And I was-- until it was possible, I was listening to the BBC until March, 1944. And I was absolutely convinced that they lost the war. The things that I was concerned is-- number one, how the war will pass me by. How I will get from one side to another. And how I'm going to survive that. And secondly, to a lesser extent-- because I did not know the real dangers we were facing-- how I will manage with the fascists. But that was only the second of my concerns, not knowing the full extent of their design for us. And though I constantly was more careful with war events-- now when the Hungarian Fascists took over, I could see the danger because they were killing people in front of me. They were killing people not moving fast enough in the road. They were killing them then they stepped out of the road. So the real danger was visible. And of course, I also knew that they were killing people by taking whole apartment houses to the Danube River and shooting them. And I knew that because we rescued a few people who survived there and took them in the hospital. And they told us what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was fully aware at the very end. I would say only the last two months, I knew they were out to kill us. But previously I didn't know. Tell me about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberation was a-- a very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] interesting event. Almost anti-climactic because we knew that the Russian army's moving closer and closer. We had to move on the street, our team. We had many losses as a result of that. But we had to be on the street to pick up wounded. And we also picked up the dead. That was our responsibility. And for just three days prior to liberation, I met a policeman who eventually were pressed into military service in-- a few blocks away from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him, how is the situation? He said this, they just beat me out of the [? Abbazia ?] coffeehouse. It almost sounded like a-- a-- a brawl. And this was already less than two kilometers. If I would make a local comparison, this was as far from me as I can come from here. So I knew that they were very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when they came, this last part of Budapest was evacuated by the Germans. They realized that they cannot resist. And they had a strong point that they wanted to use for resistance, which was just the opposite house to us where my father's store was and where my-- where our home was. And this house, with everything in it-- the whole block-- was burned in the last day. So they evacuated it. And the first thing-- we were guarding our house already, so I was on duty and watched as the Germans were leaving at the beginning of the night on the 16th to 17th. And around 5 o'clock in the morning, the Russians came in through the basements. You see, at the end of the war, you could not go out at all. It was such a heavy artillery barrage. So we broke from one air raid shelter to another. They were built in such a fashion that underground you could move. And they used this place to come in. And they came in. Were very poorly dressed people, just like the German propaganda showed it. Somebody who had seen them, for example, hugged the first Russian soldier who came and he hugged him back and took his watch. Just as they said it on their posters-- the Germans-- and, you know, they just came in. And the Russian soldiers were-- the first front-line soldiers did nothing-- occupied the place. The next wave did a great deal of opening up stores, robbing everything that was in there. Not so for themselves, because they distributed it amongst bystanders. They were the ones who attacked all the women that were available and raped them, except in our hospital. Because the Russian military commander, as soon as he discovered that there's a hospital, they took possession. They left our fascist chief to be in charge of the hospital. But instead of the fascist paraphernalia, we put up the sickle and the hammer. And everybody changed armbands. We didn't put Russian armbands. We put the red cross. The fascist uniforms were out. And the hospital continued to function and treated the same way as before. But even the Russian wounded came there out of street. And then our personnel looked after them. And we immediately received new supplies from the Russian headquarter. And the hospital continued functioning. Except our chief was arrested in the sixth day of occupation because he exhibited some loyalty to his former comrades. And he began to hide fascists in the hospital, instead of the Jews. And the-- Russian military police found some people who they were looking for in there and they arrested him.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take another short break.
